content.text: Normalisation projects a vector onto the unit hypersphere — every vector is scaled so its magnitude (L2 norm) equals 1. For embedding similarity, normalisation is not optional polish: it makes dot-product search equal to cosine similarity, ensures numerical stability, and is required before most vector databases and FAISS indexes can produce correct rankings.

import numpy as np

v = np.array([3.0, 4.0])
norm = np.linalg.norm(v)        # 5.0
v_normalised = v / norm         # [0.6, 0.8]
print(np.linalg.norm(v_normalised))  # 1.0

# Batch normalisation — efficient with broadcasting
vecs = np.random.randn(1000, 384).astype(np.float32)
norms = np.linalg.norm(vecs, axis=1, keepdims=True)  # (1000, 1)
vecs_norm = vecs / norms                              # (1000, 384)
print(np.linalg.norm(vecs_norm, axis=1).mean())       # 1.0000
Normalising at Encode Time vs. Index Time
The recommended approach is to normalise once at encode time and store the normalised vectors:

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np, faiss

model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-m3")

docs = ["cats are small carnivores", "dogs are loyal companions"]

# Option 1: normalise in the encode call (recommended)
vecs = model.encode(docs, normalize_embeddings=True)  # shape (2, 1024), ‖v‖ = 1

# Option 2: normalise manually after encoding
vecs_raw = model.encode(docs)                                      # raw
norms = np.linalg.norm(vecs_raw, axis=1, keepdims=True)
vecs_manual = vecs_raw / norms

# Both produce identical results
print(np.allclose(vecs, vecs_manual))  # True

# Store in FAISS with dot-product index (== cosine after normalisation)
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(1024)
index.add(vecs.astype(np.float32))
FAISS can normalise vectors automatically with
, which normalises in-place. This is useful when you receive vectors that haven't been normalised yet.

import faiss, numpy as np

vecs = np.random.randn(100, 1024).astype(np.float32)
print(np.linalg.norm(vecs[0]))   # e.g. 31.4 — not normalised

faiss.normalize_L2(vecs)         # in-place, modifies vecs
print(np.linalg.norm(vecs[0]))   # 1.0

# Also normalise the query before searching
query = np.random.randn(1, 1024).astype(np.float32)
faiss.normalize_L2(query)

index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(1024)
index.add(vecs)
scores, ids = index.search(query, k=5)
print(scores)  # all values in [-1, 1] since both sides are unit normalised

Matryoshka representation learning (MRL) models (OpenAI text-embedding-3, nomic-embed-text-v1.5) train vectors so any prefix of the full-dimension vector is also a meaningful embedding. When using a truncated dimension, normalise the truncated vector separately — the prefix is NOT already unit-normalised.

from openai import OpenAI
import numpy as np

client = OpenAI()

response = client.embeddings.create(
    model="text-embedding-3-small",
    input=["Matryoshka dolls nest inside each other."],
)
full_vec = np.array(response.data[0].embedding)   # 1536-dim, already unit-normalised
print(np.linalg.norm(full_vec))   # 1.0

# Truncate to 256 dims — the prefix is NOT unit-normalised
truncated = full_vec[:256]
print(np.linalg.norm(truncated))   # ~0.41 (some norm is in the dropped dims)

# Must re-normalise after truncation
truncated_norm = truncated / np.linalg.norm(truncated)
print(np.linalg.norm(truncated_norm))   # 1.0

Failure to normalise when using an inner-product index causes high-magnitude vectors to dominate rankings regardless of semantic relevance — a long document ranks higher than a short one on the same topic, simply because its embedding has larger magnitude.

import numpy as np, faiss
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-m3")

# Short and long versions of the same content
short_doc = "cats are small carnivores"
long_doc = ("Cats are small carnivorous mammals. They are obligate carnivores,"
            " meaning they require nutrients found only in animal flesh. "
            "Cats have been domesticated for approximately 9,500 years and are among "
            "the most popular domestic pets in the world.")
query = "feline pets"

vecs_raw = model.encode([short_doc, long_doc]).astype(np.float32)
norms = np.linalg.norm(vecs_raw, axis=1)
print(f"Short doc norm: {norms[0]:.2f}")  # e.g. 0.94
print(f"Long doc norm:  {norms[1]:.2f}")  # e.g. 0.98 — slightly larger

# Difference is subtle for most models since they use LayerNorm internally.
# But for bag-of-words or TF-IDF embeddings, the effect is extreme.
# Always normalise — it's cheap and never hurts.
(divide by sum of absolute values) — makes the vector a probability distribution. Used in topic models and some sparse representations. Not useful for dense embedding similarity.

— subtract the mean vector of the corpus before L2 normalisation. Can improve nearest-neighbour recall when embeddings have a strong common component (isotropic bias). Rarely used in practice for text.
— rotate and scale to make the covariance matrix the identity. Occasionally used in research to improve isotropy of embedding spaces, but complex to implement and rarely worth the effort for RAG.

Embedding models produce raw vectors whose magnitude carries no semantic meaning. A long document and a short document about identical topics may produce embeddings of very different magnitudes. Without normalisation, an inner-product similarity search ranks high-magnitude vectors preferentially — a long document about cats scores higher than a short document about cats even when the short document is more relevant to the query. Normalisation removes this magnitude bias, ensuring that only the direction (semantic content) affects similarity scores.
More concretely: if you use FAISS with
(inner product) without normalising, the score between two vectors is
factor means a document with twice the embedding magnitude gets twice the score for the same semantic similarity
exactly. The ranking then reflects semantic similarity alone.

A secondary benefit of normalisation is numerical stability. Raw embeddings from transformer models span a wide dynamic range — values can be in the range [-10, 10] depending on the model and the input. Normalised vectors are always in [-1, 1], preventing floating-point overflow in dot-product computation and making scores directly interpretable as cosine similarities.
Normalised inner product equals cosine similarity — only direction, not magnitude, determines ranking. Short and long documents are compared fairly.
After normalisation, inner-product scores are bounded to [-1, 1]: 1 means identical direction, 0 means orthogonal, -1 means opposite.
Enables the most efficient FAISS indexes.
with normalised vectors is both faster and more accurate than
for semantic search. Many approximate indexes (IVF, HNSW) also assume unit-norm vectors.
MRL models encode meaningful embeddings in prefix subspaces, but the prefix is not unit-normalised — you must re-normalise after truncation or scores will be wrong.
Normalisation of a batch of 1,000 × 1,024-dim vectors takes under 1ms on CPU. The reliability benefit is enormous relative to the cost.
If you need the embedding magnitude for any purpose (e.g., anomaly detection based on embedding confidence), normalisation destroys it. Store the original magnitudes separately if needed.

If you are computing Euclidean distance (L2) between embeddings for clustering (e.g., k-means), normalising before clustering projects all points onto the unit sphere and may collapse cluster separability for some datasets.
In-place FAISS normalisation modifies the input array.
mutates the array in-place. If you need the original vectors for other purposes, copy before normalising.
Per-truncated-prefix re-normalisation is easy to forget.
With MRL models, every time you change the truncation dimension, you must re-normalise. This is a common source of silent score degradation.
by dividing by its Euclidean norm:
The key geometric identity that makes normalisation useful for similarity search is:

After normalisation, the dot product of two unit vectors equals their cosine similarity — the measure of semantic similarity we actually want. This is why normalised vectors can use the fast inner-product index (
) without loss of accuracy relative to cosine similarity search.
Numerically, normalisation is implemented as a single vector division: for a batch of vectors of shape
with keepdims=True, then broadcast-divide. This operation has time complexity
and is implemented as a fused BLAS kernel — for practical batch sizes it is faster than any subsequent search operation.
Using inner-product (dot-product) indexes or similarity functions — without normalisation, results are not cosine similarity.
Comparing embeddings from different document lengths — magnitude varies with length in many models.
Truncating MRL model embeddings to a lower dimension.
Storing embeddings in a vector database that uses inner product as its metric (Pinecone, Weaviate default, pgvector with <=> operator).
Using L2 distance (Euclidean) for clustering — L2 distance does not benefit from normalisation the way cosine similarity does.
The embedding model guarantees unit-norm output and you have not post-processed the vectors.
Using a vector database that normalises internally (some do, some don't — check the documentation).
returns values close to 1.0 after normalisation before inserting into the index.
A common bug is normalising the corpus but not the query. Both must be unit-normalised for correct cosine similarity.
Slice the vector to the desired dimension, then call

import numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import faiss

model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-m3")

# === Correct implementation ===
corpus = [
    "Python is a high-level programming language.",
    "NumPy provides efficient array operations.",
    "FAISS is a library for efficient similarity search.",
]
query = "Which library handles vector search?"

# Encode and normalise corpus
corpus_vecs = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
# Verify: all norms must be 1.0
assert np.allclose(np.linalg.norm(corpus_vecs, axis=1), 1.0), "Corpus not normalised!"

# Encode and normalise query (same step)
query_vec = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
assert np.allclose(np.linalg.norm(query_vec), 1.0), "Query not normalised!"

# Build inner-product index (equals cosine after normalisation)
dim = corpus_vecs.shape[1]
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(dim)
index.add(corpus_vecs)

# Search — scores are cosine similarities in [-1, 1]
scores, ids = index.search(query_vec, k=3)
print("Results (cosine similarity):")
for score, idx in zip(scores[0], ids[0]):
    print(f"  {score:.4f}  {corpus[idx]}")

# === Verify inner product == cosine similarity ===
ip = corpus_vecs[0] @ query_vec[0]   # inner product of two unit vectors
cos = np.dot(corpus_vecs[0], query_vec[0])  # cosine similarity formula
assert abs(ip - cos) < 1e-6, "Inner product should equal cosine for unit vectors"
print(f"\nInner product ({ip:.4f}) == cosine similarity ({cos:.4f}): True")
Real-World Example: Production RAG Pipeline with Normalisation Checks

This example shows a production-ready RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipeline with normalisation at every critical step: corpus encoding, query encoding, and MRL truncation for a two-stage retrieval system (coarse → fine).

import numpy as np
import faiss
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

# Two-stage retrieval: fast coarse search at 256 dims, slow fine search at 1024 dims
model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-m3")  # 1024-dim MRL model


def encode_and_normalize(texts: list[str], truncate_dim: int | None = None) -> np.ndarray:
    """Encode texts, optionally truncate to MRL subdimension, and normalise."""
    vecs = model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
    if truncate_dim is not None:
        vecs = vecs[:, :truncate_dim]
        # CRITICAL: re-normalise after truncation — prefix is not unit-norm
        norms = np.linalg.norm(vecs, axis=1, keepdims=True)
        vecs = vecs / norms
    # Invariant check — never skip this in production
    norms_check = np.linalg.norm(vecs, axis=1)
    assert np.allclose(norms_check, 1.0, atol=1e-5), (
        f"Normalisation failed: min={norms_check.min():.4f}, max={norms_check.max():.4f}"
    )
    return vecs


# Build dual indexes
corpus = [
    "Transformers use self-attention mechanisms to process sequences in parallel.",
    "RNNs process sequences step by step, maintaining a hidden state.",
    "Gradient descent optimizes model weights by following the negative gradient.",
    "Batch normalization stabilizes training by normalizing layer inputs.",
    "Dropout randomly zeros activations during training to prevent overfitting.",
    "The Adam optimizer adapts learning rates using first and second moment estimates.",
]

corpus_coarse = encode_and_normalize(corpus, truncate_dim=256)  # fast 256-dim
corpus_fine = encode_and_normalize(corpus)                       # precise 1024-dim

index_coarse = faiss.IndexFlatIP(256)
index_fine = faiss.IndexFlatIP(1024)
index_coarse.add(corpus_coarse)
index_fine.add(corpus_fine)


def two_stage_search(query: str, coarse_k: int = 4, fine_k: int = 2) -> list[dict]:
    """Stage 1: fast coarse retrieval. Stage 2: re-rank with full-dim vectors."""
    q_coarse = encode_and_normalize([query], truncate_dim=256)
    coarse_scores, coarse_ids = index_coarse.search(q_coarse, k=coarse_k)
    candidates = coarse_ids[0].tolist()

    # Re-rank candidates with full-dim vectors
    q_fine = encode_and_normalize([query])
    candidate_vecs = corpus_fine[candidates]
    fine_scores = (candidate_vecs @ q_fine[0]).tolist()

    ranked = sorted(zip(fine_scores, candidates), reverse=True)[:fine_k]
    return [{"score": s, "text": corpus[i]} for s, i in ranked]


results = two_stage_search("How do optimizers work?")
for r in results:
    print(f"  {r['score']:.4f}  {r['text']}")
Mistake 1: Normalising corpus but forgetting the query

import numpy as np, faiss
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-m3")
corpus = ["cats", "dogs", "fish"]

# WRONG: corpus normalised, query not normalised
corpus_vecs = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
query_vec = model.encode(["feline pets"]).astype(np.float32)  # raw, not normalised
# Inner product now equals: ||query|| * cos(theta) — magnitude contaminates score

# RIGHT: normalise both
corpus_vecs = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
query_vec = model.encode(["feline pets"], normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
# Inner product now exactly equals cosine similarity
Mistake 2: Forgetting to re-normalise after MRL truncation

import numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-m3")
vecs = model.encode(["example text"], normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
print(np.linalg.norm(vecs[0]))  # 1.0 — correct

# WRONG: truncate and use without re-normalising
vecs_256 = vecs[:, :256]
print(np.linalg.norm(vecs_256[0]))  # ~0.40 — NOT unit norm!
# Inner product with another truncated-but-not-renorm vector is NOT cosine similarity

# RIGHT: re-normalise after truncation
vecs_256_norm = vecs_256 / np.linalg.norm(vecs_256, axis=1, keepdims=True)
print(np.linalg.norm(vecs_256_norm[0]))  # 1.0 — correct
Mistake 3: Using faiss.normalize_L2 without copying first

import numpy as np, faiss

vecs = np.random.randn(100, 512).astype(np.float32)
original_vecs = vecs  # WRONG: this is a reference, not a copy

faiss.normalize_L2(vecs)  # mutates vecs in-place
# Now original_vecs is also normalised — magnitude is lost!

# RIGHT: copy if you need the originals
original_vecs = vecs.copy()  # independent copy
faiss.normalize_L2(vecs)     # only vecs is modified
print(np.linalg.norm(vecs[0]))          # 1.0
print(np.linalg.norm(original_vecs[0]))  # original magnitude preserved
Normalise at encode time, not at search time.
If you normalise at encode time and store normalised vectors, every search operation is cheaper. If you normalise at search time, you pay the normalisation cost on every query.
Add an invariant assertion after every normalisation step.
takes microseconds and catches normalisation bugs before they corrupt your index.
Use normalize_embeddings=True in SentenceTransformers, not manual post-normalisation.
The library applies normalisation at the correct point in the pipeline (after pooling, before returning).
Document the normalisation contract for your vector store.
If your index expects normalised vectors, state this in the index initialisation comment. Future maintainers will thank you.
Use IndexFlatIP (not IndexFlatL2) for cosine similarity with normalised vectors.
L2 distance between unit vectors equals
— a monotone transformation, so ranking is the same, but inner-product is faster and scores are directly interpretable as cosine values.
For production systems, test normalisation with a regression fixture.
Store a known vector pair, their expected cosine similarity, and assert the actual inner-product score matches within tolerance on every deploy.
L2 normalisation of a batch of

operations: compute norms in one pass, divide in a second pass. For 1M × 1024-dim float32 vectors (4GB of raw data), normalisation takes approximately 800ms on a modern CPU and under 100ms on a GPU. This is a one-time cost at index build time and is negligible compared to the cost of generating embeddings (typically 100–1000x more expensive).
At query time, the query vector is normalised once before each search. For a 1024-dim query, this costs
FLOPs — less than 1μs, unmeasureable relative to network latency. There is no performance argument for skipping query normalisation.

Approximate indexes (IVF, HNSW) generally assume unit-normalised vectors. Using un-normalised vectors with these indexes degrades recall: the centroid assignment in IVF is based on L2 distance, which is not equivalent to cosine distance for un-normalised vectors, causing incorrect cluster membership and reduced recall at the same nprobe setting.
Applies after pooling; recommended approach for all SentenceTransformer models
Modifies array in-place; copy first if original needed; IndexFlatIP assumes unit norm
Can normalise on ingest; verify whether the index metric is cosine or dot product
Normalises internally when distance metric is cosine; verify for dot-product metric
Use <=> for cosine distance; must normalise vectors yourself before storing
text-embedding-3-* returns unit-norm vectors; no manual normalisation needed for full-dim use
Reference implementation; use for debugging and for environments without FAISS
Dense semantic search — the standard choice
Same ranking only if all vecs equal magnitude
Avoid for retrieval unless magnitudes are meaningful
Feature scaling for tabular ML, not embeddings
Research: improving isotropy of embedding spaces

Normalisation is a correctness invariant, not a safety mechanism — but violating it silently corrupts search results, which can have downstream consequences. In a RAG system, wrong retrieval leads to wrong answers; in a recommendation system, wrong similarity leads to wrong recommendations. Enforce the normalisation invariant with assertions at every index insertion and query point.

Numerical edge case: a zero vector (all elements zero) has L2 norm of 0, making normalisation undefined (division by zero). This can occur if a model receives empty or very short input that produces a degenerate embedding. Guard against it:

import numpy as np

def safe_normalize(vecs: np.ndarray, eps: float = 1e-10) -> np.ndarray:
    """Normalise vectors, handling zero-vectors by leaving them as-is."""
    norms = np.linalg.norm(vecs, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    # Avoid division by zero for degenerate vectors
    safe_norms = np.where(norms < eps, 1.0, norms)
    normalised = vecs / safe_norms
    # Flag zero vectors for investigation — they indicate bad input
    zero_mask = (norms.squeeze() < eps)
    if zero_mask.any():
        import warnings
        warnings.warn(f"{zero_mask.sum()} zero vectors detected — check input quality")
    return normalised

In production embedding pipelines, log the distribution of pre-normalisation norms as a monitoring metric. A sudden shift in norm distribution (e.g., norms dropping from ~1.0 to ~0.1) indicates a change in the embedding model, a preprocessing bug, or a data quality issue — all of which require investigation before the corrupted embeddings reach the index.
L2 normalisation projects vectors onto the unit sphere so that inner product equals cosine similarity — this is the foundation of correct semantic search.
Always normalise both corpus vectors and query vectors; normalising only one side produces incorrect similarity scores.
Matryoshka (MRL) model prefixes are not unit-normalised — re-normalise every time you truncate to a lower dimension.
mutates the input array in-place — copy first if you need the original magnitudes.
— scores are directly interpretable as cosine similarities in [-1, 1].
Add assertion checks after every normalisation step in production code; silent normalisation failures corrupt all subsequent similarity scores.
The cost of normalisation is negligible (O(Nd) at index build time, O(d) per query) — there is no performance reason to skip it.
For almost all embedding use cases: use L2 normalisation, do it at encode time with normalize_embeddings=True, and use an inner-product index. The other normalisation types are specialist tools.
